title: Two-dimensional arrays and matrices

publication.changeSummary: Language-neutral DSA draft with explicit C/C++/Java/Python mapping for Two-dimensional arrays and matrices; independent algorithm, implementation, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

canonicalExplanation.summary: A two-dimensional array models rows and columns, and traversal order determines coordinates, cache behavior, and execution count.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A row-major traversal visits grid[r][c] for each column before advancing to the next row. Your goal is to implement and test a matrix or grid transformation with correct dimensions and bounds. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Readiness, algorithms and analysis; Array representation and operations; pf control nested. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Shared concept and invariant
sections.body: A two-dimensional array models rows and columns, and traversal order determines coordinates, cache behavior, and execution count. This claim is assessed independently from syntax so conceptual evidence can transfer.

sections.heading: C, C++, Java, and Python implementation map
sections.body: In C, carry pointer/array bounds and explicit storage; in C++, use span, array, string, or vector with invalidation rules; in Java, use arrays, String, and collection interfaces with checked indexes; in Python, use list and str while accounting for slicing, immutability, and object overhead.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: Rows can be jagged in some languages, C multidimensional types encode stride, and empty dimensions need explicit handling. The draft therefore rejects: Two nested loops over r rows and c columns always cost O(r+c).

includes: Traverse rectangular and ragged grids by row, column, neighborhood, diagonal and boundary.
Implement and test a matrix or grid transformation with correct dimensions and bounds.

In C, carry pointer/array bounds and explicit storage; in C++, use span, array, string, or vector with invalidation rules; in Java, use arrays, String, and collection interfaces with checked indexes; in Python, use list and str while accounting for slicing, immutability, and object overhead.
excludes: Rows can be jagged in some languages, C multidimensional types encode stride, and empty dimensions need explicit handling.

outcomes: Implement and test a matrix or grid transformation with correct dimensions and bounds.

examples.title: Two-dimensional arrays and matrices: language-neutral trace
examples.situation: A row-major traversal visits grid[r][c] for each column before advancing to the next row.
walkthrough: Step 1 — State the abstract input and invariant: A row-major traversal visits grid[r][c] for each column before advancing to the next row.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: Visiting every cell costs O(r*c); preserve row lengths and language-specific stride or jagged rules.
examples.result: The conceptual result follows because Visiting every cell costs O(r*c); preserve row lengths and language-specific stride or jagged rules.

walkthrough: Step 1 — Keep the same specification and invariant across all four languages.

Step 2 — Map storage, library, equality, bounds, ownership, and error rules using: In C, carry pointer/array bounds and explicit storage; in C++, use span, array, string, or vector with invalidation rules; in Java, use arrays, String, and collection interfaces with checked indexes; in Python, use list and str while accounting for slicing, immutability, and object overhead.
Step 3 — Retest this boundary in the selected implementation: Rows can be jagged in some languages, C multidimensional types encode stride, and empty dimensions need explicit handling.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Two nested loops over r rows and c columns always cost O(r+c).
misconceptions.correction: Visiting every cell costs O(r*c); preserve row lengths and language-specific stride or jagged rules.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Use an invariant or counterexample to explain why this DSA belief fails: Two nested loops over r rows and c columns always cost O(r+c).
